title: Bay Area tech has cut 9,284 jobs in the first half of 2026
subtitle: The total has already doubled the same period a year ago, with ServiceNow, Salesforce, Ubisoft, Quizlet, and Verily Health adding 370 cuts in the latest wave.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Bay Area tech companies have eliminated 9,284 positions in the first half of 2026, already nearly double the roughly 4,700 cut in the same window of 2025, according to layoff trackers aggregated this weekend. The latest 370 cuts cover five companies on different parts of the stack: ServiceNow trimming an ITSM team, Salesforce reducing Agentforce, MuleSoft, and Marketing Cloud headcount by about 86, Ubisoft cutting 380 game-studio roles, Quizlet, and Verily Health. The pattern is consistent across the wave — each company's explanation cites AI-driven productivity gains and a shift toward 'AI-focused skills.'

What separates 2026 from previous tech contractions is that the layoffs are no longer mostly about over-hiring during the 2021 bubble. Salesforce's earlier cut of roughly 4,000 customer-support roles, with CEO Marc Benioff stating that AI agents now handle about 50% of customer interactions, is the cleanest example of a function being directly automated rather than re-organized. ServiceNow's framing — 'managing headcount with discipline while investing in AI-focused skills' — is the corporate-comms version of the same trade.

The macro picture is messier than the headline. Q1 set a venture record at $300 billion globally with AI taking ~80%, and Bay Area infrastructure firms are hiring aggressively for ML, infra, and forward-deployed engineering roles. What's compressing is the middle layer: support, sales engineering, QA, and entry-level software roles where a foundation model plus a thin agent harness now closes a real share of the work. The local job market has bifurcated, and the cumulative H1 number is what it looks like when that bifurcation runs for six months.

Takeaway for learners: if you are early in a software career, the safest thing to optimize for is the work the agents currently can't close — system design under real constraints, debugging in complex codebases, interaction with non-technical stakeholders, and ownership of outcomes rather than tasks. The opposite end — turning Jira tickets into PRs at modest complexity — is exactly what the agent stack got good at this year, and the labor-market signal is now matching that.
